Name: curvatureawareprecisionallocation
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Allocation for Resource-Efficient Neural Network Inference
Short Hypothesis: The local curvature and spectral properties of hidden activations can serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling dynamic precision allocation that optimizes computational efficiency and model accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works have explored mixed-precision inference and curvature-aware optimization, but they often rely on static profiling or costly calibration. Mix-GEMM [1] focuses on hardware-software co-design for mixed-precision GEMM operations, while SR-CurvANN [3] uses curvature-aware neural networks for 3D surface reconstruction. Tri-Accel [6] and SQR [10] propose dynamic precision and memory management, but they do not explicitly link curvature and spectral properties to quantization sensitivity. Our proposal builds on these ideas by providing a training-free, curvature-aware framework that dynamically allocates precision based on real-time activation geometry.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference offers significant latency and memory gains but often relies on static profiling or costly calibration, which fails to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Allocation (CAPA), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. CAPA computes the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states using randomized SVD, mapping these diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. Theoretical grounding with PAC-Bayes bounds links spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPA achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines, providing a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Compute the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states using randomized SVD for a variety of neural network architectures (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet) and datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
Develop a precision-switching policy based on the computed diagnostics, dynamically allocating precision levels (e.g., FP16, FP32) to different layers or tokens.
Evaluate the performance of CAPA on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, comparing it to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines in terms of throughput, accuracy, and memory usage.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature and spectral properties on quantization sensitivity, ensuring that the proposed diagnostics are robust and reliable.

Provide theoretical analysis using PAC-Bayes bounds to link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise, validating the framework's mathematical transparency and reproducibility.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of real-time spectral diagnostics may introduce additional latency, which needs to be carefully managed to ensure strict latency budgets.
The precision-switching policy may not generalize well to all neural network architectures and datasets, requiring further validation and tuning.
The theoretical analysis using PAC-Bayes bounds assumes certain conditions that may not hold in all practical scenarios, limiting the scope of the framework's applicability.
The framework's performance may be sensitive to the choice of hyperparameters, such as the number of top principal components used for spectral diagnostics.
